items.q: What determines the size of the fiscal multiplier?
items.correct: Mainly the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) — the higher the share of extra income people spend domestically, the bigger the multiplier. Leakages (saving, imports, taxes) reduce the multiplier.
items.misconception: The fiscal multiplier is always 2 or 3.
items.random_wrong: The fiscal multiplier is the rate of growth of the money supply.
items.why: The multiplier varies with conditions — bigger in slumps with high MPC and slack capacity, smaller at full employment or in open economies. Students learn a fixed number rather than the determining mechanism.

items.q: What is the difference between a shift in demand and a movement along the demand curve?
items.correct: A movement along the curve is caused by a change in price; a shift of the whole curve is caused by changes in other factors (income, tastes, substitute prices).
items.misconception: A change in price shifts the demand curve.
items.random_wrong: Demand always falls when supply increases.
items.why: This is the single most common error in introductory economics. Price changes cause movements ALONG the curve; the curve itself shifts only when non-price factors change.

items.q: What is the principle of comparative advantage?
items.correct: Countries should specialise in producing goods with lower opportunity cost relative to others — even a country worse at everything still has a comparative advantage in something.
items.misconception: A country should produce whatever it can make best.
items.random_wrong: Comparative advantage means richer countries should help poorer ones.
items.why: Students confuse absolute and comparative advantage. The Ricardian insight is that mutually beneficial trade is possible even when one country is absolutely better at everything — based on opportunity cost.

items.q: Free movement of labour and capital is added at which step?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Common market. “FTA” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Switching trade from a cheap non-member to a costly member is:
items.why: Correct answer: Trade diversion. “Trade creation” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year was the WTO founded?
items.why: Exact answer: 1995. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
